worth_it_explanation: Ad quality is about how the creatives behave once they serve, not just whether the advertiser is on-brand. A heavy creative slows the page and drains a mobile battery, and Chrome can pull it mid-load, which costs the impression and degrades Core Web Vitals at the same time. A malicious creative that auto-redirects or runs harmful code reaches readers before it is caught. The settings that limit both, the ad experiences controls, encryption enforcement, and a working path to report a bad ad, take an afternoon to review and protect every page that serves an ad.
intro: Ad quality covers the technical and security behavior of the creatives that serve through Google Ad Manager (GAM), not only their subject matter. Programmatic demand can deliver creatives that are large in file size, heavy on the processor, or outright malicious, and each of those reaches the reader even when the advertiser looks reputable. These checks confirm that heavy and disruptive ads are actively limited and that the defenses against malvertising and policy-violating creatives are in place and being used.

audit_checks.title: Heavy or disruptive ads are not controlled
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Heavy creatives slow the page, drain mobile batteries, and can be unloaded by Chrome's heavy ad intervention, which loses the impression and worsens Core Web Vitals. Leaving the disruptive-ad controls off lets programmatic demand degrade the reader experience on the publisher's own pages.

educate.base:
A creative is heavy when it pulls a large amount of bandwidth or runs the processor hard, usually from uncompressed images, video that loads before any interaction, or scripts that do heavy work in the background. The reader pays for that in a slower page and, on a phone, in battery. Chrome's heavy ad intervention watches each ad frame, and an ad that has not been interacted with is unloaded once it passes any one threshold, a value Google documents as more than 4 megabytes of network use, more than 15 seconds of main-thread time in any 30-second window, or more than 60 seconds of main-thread time in total. When that happens the reader sees an 'Ad removed' box, the impression is lost, and the layout shift counts against Core Web Vitals.

Disruptive formats are a related problem. Auto-redirecting ads, ads that expand or play sound without a gesture, and pop-ups break the reading experience and push readers toward ad blockers. Google Ad Manager exposes ad experiences controls that set protection defaults for programmatic demand across Open Auction, Private Auction, First Look, Preferred Deal, and Programmatic Guaranteed, and the ad content rules under Protections let a publisher block formats and behaviors they do not want. Leaving these at a permissive default lets programmatic demand serve whatever clears the auction.

A few signs point to weak control over heavy or disruptive ads:
  • The ad experiences controls sit at their permissive defaults with no blocks applied
  • Reporting shows creatives unloaded by the heavy ad intervention, or readers report ads that redirect or auto-play
  • The heaviest creatives by file size or load time have never been reviewed
You may want to start from the worst-performing creatives by weight and confirm the ad experiences controls reflect the formats the site is willing to serve. The goal is to limit the creatives that cost the reader before Chrome does it for you.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, the heavy ad intervention removing creatives mid-load shows up as lost revenue paired with a worse layout-shift score, so the cost lands on yield and on Core Web Vitals at once. You may want to review the ad experiences controls against the formats your audience tolerates and confirm the heaviest creatives are not the ones Chrome is unloading.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a heavy or auto-playing ad competes with the product or checkout flow for the reader's device, and a slow page costs sessions that were close to converting. You may want to keep the disruptive-ad controls strict on pages that carry both editorial content and a path to purchase.

investigate.intro: Review the ad experiences controls and the ad content protections, then look at the worst-performing creatives by weight to see whether heavy or disruptive ads are reaching readers.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Protections', then the ad experiences controls, and note whether the settings sit at their permissive defaults or have blocks applied for the formats the site does not want to serve.
Open the ad content rules under Protections and review which categories, formats, and behaviors are blocked for programmatic demand.
Open reporting and sort creatives or line items by a weight or load metric where available, and note the heaviest creatives serving on the site.

google_publisher_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Load a representative page and open the Google Publisher Console from the page, then inspect the ad slots and the creatives that filled them.
Note any slot whose creative is large in file size or slow to render, and reproduce on a throttled mobile profile to see whether the experience degrades.

chrome_devtools.label: Chrome DevTools
steps: Open a page that serves ads, open DevTools, and watch the Console for any heavy ad intervention report or 'Ad removed' message.
Use the Network and Performance panels to identify ad frames that pull large payloads or hold the main thread, and record the creatives behind them.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the ad experiences controls and ad content protections are set to limit unwanted formats, whether reporting or DevTools shows creatives unloaded by the heavy ad intervention, and whether the heaviest creatives have been reviewed. A setup with protections applied to the unwanted formats and no creatives being unloaded passes. Permissive defaults with heavy or disruptive creatives reaching readers is a fail, recorded with the formats or creatives at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the ad experiences control settings and the heaviest creatives observed above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that heavy and disruptive ads are not actively controlled on {{site_name}}. The ad experiences controls and ad content protections sit at permissive defaults, so programmatic demand can serve creatives that are large in file size or run the processor hard. Chrome's heavy ad intervention unloads such creatives once they pass its resource thresholds, which loses the impression and adds a layout shift that counts against Core Web Vitals. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the ad experiences controls against the formats the site is willing to serve, blocking the disruptive behaviors under the ad content protections, and checking the heaviest creatives by weight so the worst offenders are caught before Chrome removes them.

audit_checks.title: Defenses against malvertising and policy-violating creatives are weak
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Programmatic demand can carry creatives that auto-redirect, run malicious code, or violate policy, and weak defenses let those reach readers before they are caught. A single malicious creative damages reader trust and exposes the publisher to the harm it does on the page.

educate.base:
Malvertising is a creative that does harm rather than just advertising something, by auto-redirecting the reader to a scam page, running code that attacks the browser, or calling out to domains tied to malware. Because programmatic demand fills inventory automatically, a publisher does not pick these creatives, so the defenses have to catch them. Google Ad Manager (GAM) scans creatives with its own malware detection tools and pulls any creative found to carry malware, and it forbids auto-redirects, pop-ups, and fourth-party calls to uncertified vendors. Those defenses reduce the exposure, but they work best when the publisher's own settings reinforce them.

Encryption is the first control. Creatives that serve on a secure page have to be secure as well, or the browser shows a mixed-content warning or fails to render the ad. Google Ad Manager scans creatives for Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) compliance, marks them compliant or non-compliant, and the publisher can require that only compliant creatives serve. A creative hosted on an insecure third-party server is the common cause of a non-compliant status. Requiring encryption keeps those off secure pages.

Creative review is the second control. The Ad review center lets a publisher see individual ads after they have shown and choose whether to keep showing them, which is the place to catch a creative that slipped through the automated scan. Reviewing the ads that actually served, rather than trusting the auction alone, closes the gap between what the scan catches and what a reader reports.

The reporting path is the third control. When a bad ad does reach a reader, the publisher needs a way to capture it and get it blocked. A few questions confirm the path is real:
  • Is encryption required so only SSL-compliant creatives serve
  • Is the Ad review center being used to review and block creatives that served
  • Is there a known process to report a malicious or policy-violating ad and have it pulled
You may want to confirm all three are in place. A scan alone leaves the publisher waiting on Google to catch what reaches the page, with no control of their own.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, a malicious ad that redirects a reader or triggers a malware warning damages the trust the audience has in the publication, and that trust is harder to rebuild than the lost impression. You may want to require SSL-compliant creatives, use the Ad review center on the ads that served, and keep a clear path to report and block a bad ad.
site_type_overlays.healthcare: On a healthcare-content site, a reader who hits a scam redirect or a deceptive ad while looking for medical information is exposed at a vulnerable moment, and the reputational cost is sharper. You may want to keep encryption enforcement and creative review strict, and treat any reported malvertising as urgent.

investigate.intro: Confirm that encryption is required for serving creatives, review how creatives are being reviewed in the Ad review center, and check the process for reporting a malicious or policy-violating ad.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Inventory', then the inventory or creative settings, and note whether the network requires Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) compliance so only compliant creatives serve on secure pages.
Open the SSL compliance status on a sample of creatives and note any marked non-compliant, then check whether those are blocked from serving.
Open the Ad review center, review the ads that have served, and note whether the publisher is actively reviewing and blocking creatives rather than leaving the auction unchecked.

google_publisher_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Load a page that serves ads and open the Google Publisher Console, then inspect the creatives that filled the slots for any insecure request or unexpected redirect.
Reproduce any reported bad ad on the affected page and capture the creative or advertiser detail needed to report and block it.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether encryption is required so only SSL-compliant creatives serve, whether the Ad review center is being used to review and block served creatives, and whether a working process exists to report and block a malicious ad. A setup with encryption enforced and a real path to catch and block bad creatives passes. Insecure creatives allowed to serve, an unused Ad review center, or no reporting path is a fail, recorded with the gap at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the encryption enforcement status, the Ad review center usage, and the reporting path observed above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the defenses against malvertising and policy-violating creatives are weak on {{site_name}}. Programmatic demand can carry creatives that auto-redirect, run malicious code, or serve insecurely, and the controls that limit them are not all in place. Secure Sockets Layer compliance is not being enforced for serving creatives, the Ad review center is not being used to review what served, or there is no clear path to report and block a malicious ad. {{voice_recommend}} requiring SSL-compliant creatives so insecure ads do not serve on secure pages, using the Ad review center to review and block creatives that reach readers, and establishing a known process to report a bad ad and have it pulled quickly.
